item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that're used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from here, available under the same license, and the rest of the images were made for the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm fairly sure they're right to be paranoid by the end, and personally i love how the tone of the addenda switch so suddenly from "work out what this means because SCIENCE" to "wait we did what it wanted us to we unearthed the monolith iTS A TRAP"

(Fully aware of the necro but since the last post was just as much of one… eh)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it. This is exactly the kind of stuff I come to this wiki for: mysterious objects that we know just enough about to keep us guessing bu little enough to keep it mysterious. I also like how the SCP Foundation is getting all paranoid over it when we have no evidence that it's a threat, though their reaction is actually understandable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like, I'm a big fan of the extraterrestrial stuff here on the wiki, and this is the best I've read.

I hope you don't mind, but I fixed one typo: "released" to "realised" in the start of the third paragraph in the technical report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You may not want to explicitly state them, but I'd love to hear them through a PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article and seriously wish there were more like it. Yes, the creepy is nice and all, but the SCP Foundation is also a research facility, and this encapsulates it perfectly.
Also, great use of images that add depth without breaking the suspension of disbelief. I'm sure this could be done to great effect in other articles!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this. The amount of detail and effort in the addendum really makes this article. Loved it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was meh on this, then read the first log.
Upvoted so fucking hard my mousepad almost broke.
That log is so brilliantly done it deserves to be troll-submitted to some UFO-crazy magazine, or document-dropped somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The alien race must spend all their time and resources littering space with the things."

Perhaps they are!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for space stuff. I would've +1'd this just for being a reverse Voyager probe, but then you went in and made it seem to be a trap. Well done.

My one quibble would be that the odds of one probe sent out landing on Earth is small enough. The odds of 5? The alien race must spend all their time and resources littering space with the things.

Still, it's necessary to make the twist work so I'm willing to overlook it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly everything about this is fantastic. An interstellar fishing lure that we just bit on? Scares the hell out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hipsters, of course!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
from being at -9 to being on the 2nd page of "top rated articles" in all of 4 days?

color me impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I'm wrong in my previous post then, and even more glad that the author didn't go over the top in response to the initial negative response. (As I said, it sounded cliche as I was writing it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the change that got my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The classified addendum doesn't do much for me, but I'm glad this article is being appreciated now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think that any execution could save that idea for me. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone like myself would upvote it for cleverness and let things go from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would it ever get off of zero?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is going on my favorites list.
Comprehensive, interesting, well thought-out and wonderfully executed in my opinion. There are few SCP articles that show intelligent and methodical scientific research of the object, and this is one of them. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have my own ideas about the background of the object but I don't want to explicitly state them. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contact meets the Arecibo message. I like it. I'd like to see some Foundation Tales that use this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is spooky but more than that it immediately sent my imagination flying. There’s nothing like a good alien origin. To me that is an automatic win. I enjoyed it a great deal, keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I absolutely loved the description of the manner in which the message was encoded and how it came to be decoded.
It's very similar to the logic Sagan et al used to create a kind of universally understood message. Very similar, but not exactly the same. It uses base one counting of prime numbers and calibrates against a hydrogen atom, but then it takes a turn away.

This is a great article. When I scrolled down to see the pixel art of the biped next to the sphere for scale my heart did a little jump.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm suddenly struck by the idea of writing a -J that describes the ratings module as a memetic effect that causes people to click the button with the sign it's currently displaying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it would probably be similar to wherever The Weeping Angels are from in Doctor Who. Though i think they tend to stow away on ships rather than build them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see you made some edits, but while this hasn't honestly changed ALL that much since I read it last, apparently you changed enough to get me to like it. Vote switched to +1.

(seriously I don't know what precisely did it, though, beyond it being a bit easier to read, which is kind of weirding me out because now I feel like I'm just going with the current trend :P)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just envisioned a world where 173 is the dominant life form and has mastered space travel.
And I was so looking forward to not sitting up all night clutching a shotgun…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally the shaped SCP's are just a sphere or cube that does some random thing for no reason. Seeing how this is a transmission module made to travel through space, however, it actually makes sense for it to be shaped like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot more now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: exactly what i was going to say. though i also wanted to ask whether 1.73 is a reference to SCP-173, because the biped in the picture kinda looks like him, although i have no idea how 173 would be extraterrestrial in origen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't think a detailed tesselated star or whatever would make a believable space probe.

I'm waiting to see someone using a tesselated star just so I can rush to make a "Dangerously Kepler!" joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback. Here's my thinking about the points.

1) It's not required reading to understand the basis of the SCP. It's supposed to be somewhat grounded in reality and I thought it would make it more believable in a way.

2) Trying to make the SCP's function somewhat clearer than it was in the first draft.

3) How do you know they are smart?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
balls

I C WAT U DID THAR.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most dramatic turnarounds I've seen in an objects ranking. Wow. Good work author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Not everything has to be horror. This is pretty interesting as it is. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We already have lots horrific things. Need some more innocuous things in there for texture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so, here's the thing. Spheres are hated because they're often used as a filler shape, an abstract the writer came up with because they couldn't come up with anything better. "I can't think of anything interesting for this SCP to be, so it's going to be a sphere. Or a cube. Or some other random geometric shape."

However, there's a huge difference to just tossing in a sphere because you have no idea how to describe your SCP, and having an object that happens to be round. It's like having a basketball SCP. Of course it's a sphere. That wouldn't make it a bad SCP. A six-sided die is going to be a cube.

This is not a filler shape. This is an object that happens to be spheroid (and it makes sense for it to be spheroid). So chill, people. Take it for what it is and vote based on that, not on its shape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this so hard and I can't believe it was ever downvoted for being round.

Seriously guys, of the things that make sense to be round, alien probes are pretty high up there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You managed to make a sphere that didn't suck balls! Congratulations!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very good, and well-written.

And it is creepy. The text leads the reader down the path of interpreting the object as some extraterrestrial civilization's version of the Voyager records, but there's an interesting twist. Well done. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first draft was supposed to be unsettling, I wanted to let people fill in the blanks. The consensus was that it was boring and it quickly drifted down to -10 or so. I had tried to make something realistic and at least somewhat believable, that's why I chose a sphere and explained the signals. I didn't think a detailed tesselated star or whatever would make a believable space probe.

I got some good feedback and added the second document which is a bit more expository, I think the article is better for it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Get rid of the technical information on how the images were encoded and how it was figured out; it doesn't make the article more interesting or creepy. You could replace it with a single sentence referencing another document which isn't included on the page.

2) The second and third paragraphs in Document 1947-B are redundant, except for revealing that the radio transmission is directed towards Scorpius.

3) There being multiple other instances of the SCP which give different information than the first one gives further weight towards "they're lying to us" interpretation, but if the aliens were lying they'd be smart enough to be consistent about their lies (at least on a single planet).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spheres are… how does one say, 'cliche'. There are a great many SCP articles that are shaped like a sphere, and people tend to get on a knee jerk bandwagon against SCPs shaped like one. A lot of the time, it's a sign of unoriginality. Same with cubes. Not that there are a lot of good reasons to have spheroid or cuboid SCP objects, it's just that a lot of people use it as a crutch.

And… well, it's best to aim for creepy when writing an SCP, and a lot of people expect for you to aim for it.

… Of course, they don't take into account that a lot of great articles are not 'scary', and that not everyone can do creepy well, but they're allowed to vote as they see fit.

Again, I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is clever and subtle, and I can't for the life of me figure out why it being 1) a sphere 2) that doesn't smash me in the face with "creepiness" are considered to be bad things. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't creepy. But I think it's damn cool. I'm a sucker for alien stuff and this is awesomely put together. I love it.

EDIT : This went from +2 to +5 in the time I wrote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually got the subtle horror once it started mentioning that "None of the things we found near Scorpio match ANYTHING this probe is showing", even before I got to the other spheres (which I suppose were to make it clearer that the voyager-like images are a lie and a trap).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fucking spheres to be precise.
or FUCKING SPHERES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was a pointless expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the new document, adds a bit of doubt as to whether this thing is a genuine Voyager-esque probe or a trap, but there was no real need to expunge that information. Anyone with a little imagination can grasp that Image 2 shows the system in relation to other locations. Expungement doesn't automatically add creepiness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Zekky, it doesn't necessarily have to be "creepy" in an absolute sense, but as something anomalous that is deserving of being otherwise isolated from mankind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey guys I added some more stuff - Hopefully it's creepier now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very impressed by the ability of pixel images to creep me out. That said, I got the feeling this article was more about the researchers and other personnel than the object. Definitely potential to make this one great, a lot of the feedback from the comments has been very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lovely idea, but doesn't inspire the dread I'd hoped it would. No vote, but leaning toward +1 for me.

Nice easter egg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. Plus one from me.

I don't really see much he can change- I think the meat being in the addendum was the best way to do it. It's not as if the SCPs and description were long enough that you couldn't bring yourself to read the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was what I was going for, if anyone has any ideas as to how I can make it more clear/creepy do share.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of a reverse Voyager probe, but it does need more of a horror/creep hook to it. I especially appreciate the effort you put into the images. Upvoted for potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My God, this is excellent. Why is this at -9 again?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might I suggest that the reason all that other stuff is in there is to intrigue the alien species into trying to respond, not to actually provide information? "Oh hey there's this star map and rough biological shape and even a homeworld. They must want to be friends. Let's try to follow the directions so we can be friends/trade buddies/trick them into trusting us so we can eat them." And then the signal changes and you suddenly realize that you're dealing with the best wolf seamstress that ever sewed wool costumes.

Also guys: It is a sphere because it is a reference to a real life object. While ordinarily sphere SCPs are bad, I think that this one deserves a pass on this front because it was not laziness on the part of the author, but an attempt to draw the reader in by using real life things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's just because I love space-related SCPs, but I actually like this. I like the idea of us discovering a Voyager equivalent. I don't mind that it's a sphere. I like the effort that you seem to have put into the images. This doesn't really deserve to be downvoted like it is. SCPs don't have to be creepy; they can be interesting instead. I found this interesting. +1
